journal_entry.description: Discounting of 90-day non-interest-bearing note receivable at bank (15% annual discount rate, 40 days remaining)
journal_entry.rationale: Ford discounted a $100,000 note receivable with 40 days remaining at 15% annual rate. The bank discount = $100,000 × 15% × (40/365) = $1,643.84. Net proceeds = $100,000 - $1,643.84 = $98,356.16. Since management determined this qualifies as derecognition under IFRS 9 (transfer of risks and rewards), the note receivable is removed from the books. The difference between the carrying amount ($100,000) and proceeds received ($98,356.16) is recognized as a loss on discounting ($1,643.84). Debit tuple (1,0,1,0,0,0) indicates one asset increase (Cash) and one expense increase (Loss on Discounting). Credit tuple (0,0,0,1,0,0) indicates one asset decrease (Notes Receivable). No tax applies to financial transactions.

output.reason: Ford discounts the note at the bank, receiving cash (asset increase) but at a discount. The debit side records: (1) Cash received = asset increase, and (2) Loss on discounting = expense increase. The discount charge represents the financing cost/loss from selling the note before maturity, which is an expense. The credit side would derecognize the note receivable (asset decrease). Two distinct debit lines: one for the cash proceeds received and one for the loss/discount expense incurred.

debit_classifier.reason: Ford discounts the note at the bank, receiving cash (asset increase) but at a discount. The debit side records: (1) Cash received = asset increase, and (2) Loss on discounting = expense increase. The discount charge represents the financing cost/loss from selling the note before maturity, which is an expense. The credit side would derecognize the note receivable (asset decrease). Two distinct debit lines: one for the cash proceeds received and one for the loss/discount expense incurred.

output.reason: On the credit side, Ford is discounting (selling) the note receivable to the bank. Since this qualifies as derecognition under IFRS 9, Ford removes the note receivable from its books. This represents an asset decrease (the note receivable is eliminated). The difference between the face value and the net proceeds received creates a loss on discounting, which is an expense increase on the debit side, but we only classify credits here. Credit side: (1) Note receivable eliminated = asset decrease.

The difference between the face value and the net proceeds received creates a loss on discounting, which is an expense increase on the debit side, but we only classify credits here. Credit side: (1) Note receivable eliminated = asset decrease.
